questions.question: What is a data race?
options: Two threads running on different CPU cores simultaneously
Two or more threads accessing the same memory location concurrently, at least one is a write, with no synchronization
A thread that runs faster than expected and completes before another thread
A bug in the operating system's thread scheduler
questions.explanation: A data race requires three conditions: concurrent access to the same memory, at least one write, and no synchronization to order the accesses. It is undefined behavior in C and C++.

questions.question: What does it mean for an operation to be atomic?
options: It executes very quickly (within a single clock cycle)
It uses all available CPU cores
It appears to execute indivisibly from the perspective of other threads
It can be split into smaller parallel sub-operations
questions.explanation: Atomic means indivisible. Other threads either see the entire operation or none of it, never an intermediate state. For example, `counter++` is NOT atomic because it compiles to LOAD, ADD, STORE — three separate instructions.

questions.question: Thread A writes data=42 then ready=1. Thread B spins on ready then reads data. Even if B sees ready==1, why might B read data==0?
options: Thread B is reading the wrong variable
CPU caches and compiler/CPU reordering can make the write to ready visible before the write to data, unless proper memory ordering (acquire/release) is used
The variable 'data' is stored on a slower memory device
Thread A must use a printf() for the write to be visible
questions.explanation: Without synchronization, the CPU can reorder the stores (making ready=1 visible before data=42 on weak architectures like ARM/Power), the compiler can reorder the stores, or the cache coherence protocol can propagate ready before data. Acquire/release semantics establish happens-before and prevent this.

questions.question: How does Rust prevent data races at compile time when C cannot?
options: Rust runs all threads sequentially
Rust's ownership system ensures mutable data cannot be shared across threads without explicit synchronization (Mutex, atomics, etc.)
Rust adds runtime bounds checks that detect races
Rust uses a garbage collector that synchronizes all memory access
questions.explanation: Rust's ownership rules give each value either one mutable reference or multiple immutable references. When combined with Send/Sync traits, this means shared mutable state requires explicit synchronization (Arc<Mutex<>>, atomic types, etc.). The compiler rejects any code that attempts a data race. C has no equivalent safety check.

questions.question: What is a data race?
options: Two threads reading the same variable simultaneously
Two threads accessing the same memory location concurrently, with at least one writing, without synchronization
A thread finishing before another thread
A deadlock between two threads
questions.explanation: A data race occurs when: (1) two threads access the same memory, (2) at least one access is a write, and (3) no synchronization orders the accesses. This leads to undefined behavior in C/C++ and unpredictable results in any language. Both concurrent reads or synchronized access are safe.
